data.title: Amazon Looks Beyond AWS And Advertising To Improve EPS Growth
data.description:
Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) recent earnings beat has improved the sentiment towards the stock, along with better macro conditions due to recent developments in the tariff situation. However, Amazon has also shown a strong trend of improving the operating margin in the last few quarters. Most of the focus has been towards AWS and the advertising segment. Both these segments have contributed a big chunk of margin growth in the last few years. Amazon is now showing another trend that can drive margin expansion for the next decade. The rapid growth of robots within its warehouses is helping reduce hiring curve according to internal company documents. This should reduce the labor cost for the company and build sustainable margin expansion within e-commerce business. In the previous article, it was mentioned that a weaker dollar will help Amazon's EPS growth.

In the recent quarter, the shipping cost increased by 3% YoY while the worldwide paid units increased by 8%. This clearly shows that Amazon is able to deliver more packages while keeping a lid on the shipping costs. A similar trend can be seen in the last few quarters. The employee headcount growth has also been 3% YoY which is lower than the overall revenue growth and growth in paid units delivered. Amazon is also a major player in the autonomous vehicle segment through Zoox which is planning on fleet expansion in the next few quarters. Eventually, we could see more automation in warehouses and delivery which should improve Amazon’s labor cost metric.

The stock is currently trading at only 23.5 times the EPS estimate for fiscal year ending Dec 2027. This is quite modest when we look at forward EPS growth momentum. If the labor cost optimization continues, we could see further improvement in margin trajectory which can give upward revisions to EPS estimates.

Recent earnings and shipping cost optimization

Amazon was able to beat EPS estimate by $0.23 and revenue estimate by $546 million in the recent earnings. The management has given soft guidance for the next quarter but this is mostly due to macro conditions. At the same time, we have seen better trade rhetoric from White House in the last few weeks which has improved sentiment across Wall Street.

Figure: Recent earnings numbers of Amazon. Source: Seeking Alpha

Beyond the quarterly earnings, we can see a longer term positive trend within Amazon. Over the last few quarters, Amazon has been able to show lower shipping costs compared to worldwide paid units delivery. If the YoY growth in paid units is higher than shipping costs, it will help Amazon improve its overall cost structure. The retail trade is based on wafer-thin margins and high volume. Even a few percentage point improvement in cost structure could deliver a massive jump in operating income.

In Q1 2025, the shipping cost increased by 3% YoY compared to 8% growth in paid units. In Q4 2024, the shipping cost increased by 4% YoY compared to 11% growth in paid unit. Similarly, in Q3 2024 the shipping costs increased by 8% YoY compared to 12% growth in paid units. We can clearly see a 3 to 7 percentage point difference in shipping costs and paid units. At the same time, the overall employee headcount has also been quite low compared to total revenue growth in the last few quarters. Internal documents already mention that the company is seeing a flattening of hiring curve due to the use of robotics within warehouses.

Figure: Recent shipping cost and paid units growth in Amazon. Source: Company Filings

Robotics in warehouses is finally showing an impact

Amazon has been investing in robotics within its warehouses for almost a decade. Initially, robots were used to take over mundane tasks like transferring packages within the warehouse. Recently, Amazon has started building robots that can complete more complex task of sorting the packages. Amazon mentioned that its new robot called Vulcan has a “sense of touch”. Vulcan can complete stocking process which was handled by employees.

While Elon Musk has been promoting a humanoid robot, Amazon is already using thousands of robots to improve the efficiency of its warehouses. Many bullish analysts in Tesla (TSLA) also mention the possible growth of humanoid robots as a key factor. However, it is likely that the biggest benefit from humanoid or other robots will actually be in using them within a company’s operations instead of the company that manufactures them. Amazon can develop robots that exactly meet the requirements of its warehouses and this could replace the massive labor costs for the company.

Amazon has struggled with labor issues in the warehouses over the last few years. Many employees have complained about tough productivity parameters and the potential for injuries. Higher use of robots could reduce some of the more dangerous jobs in Amazon’s warehouses. We have already started seeing a more limited growth in Amazon’s overall headcount. A bulk of employees work in fulfillment and it is likely that these robots could limit the headcount growth. In the recent quarter, Amazon mentioned that it had 1.56 million employees which was a 3% YoY growth.

Zoox and possible consolidation in retail industry

Amazon is also a key player in the self-driving business with Google’s (GOOG) Waymo and Tesla being the main competitors. Tesla will be launching its highly anticipated robotaxi in June in Austin (hopefully) after years of delay. On the other hand, Waymo has reported completing 250,000 weekly paid trips. Amazon has also been investing in Zoox over the last few years. There have been trials for this technology and some recent setbacks.

Besides the obvious robotaxi service, Zoox can also be used for package delivery. Amazon has not given a roadmap for automated package delivery. But we know that Amazon is very serious in improving its logistics capability and bringing more package deliveries within the company. It is also important to note significant progress made in China in automated package delivery in the last few quarters. China has set an ambitious target of fully automating the logistics sector by 2030. This has led the government to ease regulations and support more companies that are building automated delivery systems. Chinese logistics giant ZTO Express has already launched hundreds of fully automated vehicles which can deliver millions of parcels.

Figure: Rapid growth of Amazon Logistics. Source: Voronoi

The last-mile delivery is one of the biggest factors for overall labor cost. It is highly likely that Amazon will also start investing in automated delivery as regulations become clearer over the next few quarters.

A more automated warehousing and delivery network can give Amazon a massive edge over other retailers. It can help Amazon bring down the costs and increase the market share in the retail sector. Historically, retail business has been very fragmented compared to other businesses like telecom. An automated warehouse and delivery network requires massive investment and very few companies have the resources to build similar system. This can lead to more consolidation in this segment which can help improve Amazon’s market share and improve the margin.

Even a 5%-10% decrease in labor cost can have a massive impact on Amazon’s margins and bottom line. We could see lower shipping costs in the next few quarters which can start making a big impact on Amazon’s EPS and forward estimates.

Amazon stock price is quite modest

Amazon has relied on AWS and advertising business to deliver its operating income over the last few years. The automation of its warehouse and delivery business could provide another significant driver for its operating income growth. According to EPS estimates for fiscal year ending Dec 2027, the stock is trading at 23.50.

We can see in the above chart that both Amazon and Apple are trading at almost similar price and EPS estimate for 2 fiscal years ahead. However, Amazon's EPS growth has been much higher than that of Apple. The forward EPS growth projections of Amazon are also much higher than Apple. Apple is investing heavily in buybacks which is helping to improve EPS growth. On the other hand, Amazon's EPS growth is coming from better productivity and margin expansion. This gives Amazon an edge as more automation is used within its logistics.

Figure: Forward EPS estimates of Amazon. Source: Seeking Alpha

Amazon is in a good position to take advantage of new innovations in robotics and self driving business. This will likely give the company a massive edge over its competition and it could also improve the valuation multiple for the stock.

Investor Takeaway

Amazon is showing a positive trend of lower shipping costs compared to paid units delivered. New robots are being used within Amazon’s warehouses to reduce the labor costs. This can improve the margin trajectory for the company in the next few years. Amazon is also a key player in the self driving business.

The company has not mentioned any automated delivery service. But it is likely that Amazon could start using last-mile automated delivery system as regulations become clearer. The forward PE multiple of Amazon stock is moderate when we look at other big tech peers making the stock quite attractive.

data.title: Duolingo: Sharply Overvalued And Primed To Fall
data.description:
Amid a stock market that has largely recovered its post-tariff losses, now is hardly a time for investors to be complacent. Dispersion in performance between individual stocks is likely to be high, and stocks that are riding on high valuations are overdue for a correction.

Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL), in my view, is one of these stocks. The popular language-learning app continues to defy Street expectations with its eye-watering revenue growth and consistency in adding paid subscribers. New AI-powered features, such as conversational intelligence only available in the company's highest-end Duolingo Max subscriptions, have driven great user retention and brought more paid users into the fold. And yet, we have to ask a sobering question: how much of Duolingo's strength is already priced in?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Duolingo in February, when the stock was still trading in the low $300s. Since then, Duolingo has substantially outperformed the broader market – not only recouping its tariff-correction losses but also finding new all-time highs. Amid what I believe to be a worryingly unsupportable valuation, I'm dropping my rating on Duolingo to sell.

Of course, there are credible reasons why investors are getting excited about Duolingo. One of the most meaningful near-term drivers for the company is the fact that it has adopted machine-driven translation (similar to what Reddit (RDDT) is doing) to translate its language courses into more base languages. For example, Spanish speakers now have the ability to take a course in Mandarin. A list of some of the most anticipated course launches is shown in the snapshot below:

Duolingo notes that its earlier courses took years to build out: and now, the company was able to add ~150 new courses in under a year using AI. The company is also broadening far beyond its core base of language learning: on top of math courses, the company has also introduced a new course for learning chess. The broadening of Duolingo's universe, and making its content more accessible to non-native English speakers, greatly expands Duolingo's TAM.

But we do have to ask ourselves the tough question: doesn't Duolingo's price already capture all of this potential strength (and then some)? At current share prices near $525, Duolingo trades at a market cap of $23.84 billion. After we net off the $877.7 million of cash (against zero debt) on Duolingo's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $22.96 billion.

For FY25, Duolingo has boosted its full-year guidance outlook to $1.118-$1.126 billion in revenue, representing a 28-29% y/y growth range, up ~4 points at the midpoint versus a previous 24-26% growth range (and a big driver behind Duolingo's recent rally). It also boosted the midpoint of its adjusted EBITDA margin range to 28.0%, 50bps higher than 27.5% previously.

Duolingo's valuation sits at record-high multiples of:

  • 23x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 83x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

In other words, Duolingo is trading at a richer multiple of revenue than the S&P 500 is trading as a multiple of earnings. Beyond valuation, Duolingo isn't devoid of fundamental risks as well. We note that Duolingo's CEO, Luis von Ahn, recently triggered sharp controversy by suggesting that AI could eventually replace teachers – comments that sparked broad outrage and resulted in the CEO walking back his comments. A company like Duolingo that relies on consumer loyalty can be jarred by public backlash: the real question we have to ask is, especially amid a tougher macroeconomic backdrop where international travel is pulling back, and consumer spending weakens, how sticky is a Duolingo subscription in the end?

Duolingo's >20x revenue multiple prices the company for perfection, and it's highly likely to disappoint going forward. I recommend locking in gains here and investing elsewhere.

Q1 Download

That being said, Duolingo still delivered terrific Q1 results that defied expectations on when its growth rates would eventually slow. My argument is not that Duolingo isn't executing admirably, nor that the company isn't high quality: rather, simply that Duolingo's current valuation already handsomely compensates for that quality.

The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Duolingo's revenue grew 38% y/y to $230.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $223.1 million (+33% y/y) by a five-point margin. Duolingo's revenue growth barely showed any deceleration from its 39% growth pace in the prior Q4.

The company's core user metrics also showed significant performance. Monthly active users grew 33% y/y to 130.2 million, while the company's most loyal cadre of daily active users grew at an even stronger 49% y/y pace to 46.6 million, also barely decelerating from Q4's 51% growth pace and adding a tremendous 6.1 million net-new daily users since last quarter. The company also added 0.8 million net new paid users since the end of Q4, while bookings grew 38% y/y to $271.6 million.

All of these are promising indicators for the company's plan to hit 28-29% revenue growth for the year, which now appears quite conservative with room for upside. The company notes that it has made its AI-powered features, such as Video Call with Lily (the conversational AI tool that is only available for Duolingo Max subscribers at $30/month), have driven more of its subscribers toward higher subscription tiers.

The company is optimistic about newer offerings like chess bringing new subscribers into the fold, with potential cross-sale opportunities. Per CEO Luis von Ahn's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

So we’re very happy, we just added chess. It’s not quite yet live in the app, but it’s going to be live very soon. I should mention something amazing about chess is that it really started with a team of two people, neither of whom knew how to program, so they were not programmers. And they basically made prototypes and did the whole curriculum of chess by just using AI. Also, neither of them knew how to play chess. And we started that for several months. And eventually, when they had a really good prototype, we had a whole team to professionalize it and put it in the app. So we’re very happy with it. This is, at the moment, mainly going to be to increase users. We think there as a huge demand for chess. There is hundreds of millions of people who want to play chess. And the same is true for math and music. I mean they’re growing really well. We are monetizing all of these subjects, by the way. The way you monetize them is the same way we monetize language learning, which is if you want to turn off ads, you can pay to subscribe. So we’re monetizing them already. But compared to language learning, they’re a lot smaller. But yes, we’re very excited about it."

On the profitability front, we note that Duolingo suffered a two-point headwind on gross margins, which reduced to 71.0%. We should emphasize that AI is far from free – though Duolingo has managed to upsell customers onto its Duolingo Max tier, the use of demanding AI LLMs to power these features is eating into the company's margin profile.

Still, Duolingo managed to offset this headwind with efficiencies in opex, particularly in cutting down general and administrative expenses to 13% of revenue.

As a result, Duolingo still grew adjusted EBITDA at a 43% y/y clip to $62.8 million, representing a 27.2% adjusted EBITDA margin that improved 90bps y/y. Still, we think Duolingo is still quite a while out from being able to justify its valuation from a bottom-line perspective.

Key Takeaways

Without a doubt, Duolingo is one of the surprising success stories within consumer internet applications. The company has quickly grown into the world's dominant language-learning platform and is quickly expanding its footprint by machine-translating its content into other languages and adding new content such as chess and music for people of different interests.

While I consider Duolingo to be a high-quality investment, that doesn't necessarily make it a great investment, especially at ~23x forward revenue. In my view, the company is overdue for a correction, especially because expectations for the company are already extremely high. Take caution here and use the recent sharp rally as an opportunity to lock in gains.

data.title: FMC Corporation Could Become One Of The Best Turnaround Play Of 2025
data.description:
Introduction

FMC (NYSE: NYSE:FMC) is a global agricultural giant that provides innovative and sustainable crop protection solutions. It traces its history back to 1883, when it initially developed a pump for spraying insecticides. Since, the company has drastically evolved, entering many businesses relevant to agriculture through acquisitions.

The stock has been hammered since 2023, losing more than 70% of its value due to lower sales volumes and losing more than a third of its market value when it slashed its 2025 financial forecast back in February. FMC also struggled with distribution issues in Latin America. I want to see if an investment in the company could be a great turnaround play.

Segment Overview

The company has 4 main segments consisting of Insecticides, Herbicides, Fungicides, and Plant Health.

The Insecticides segment includes key products such as Rynaxypyr and Cyazypyr, which are chemical formulations designed to control and eliminate insect pests that reduce crop yield and quality. It protects these high-value crops in regions like Latin America and North America. Sales have dipped in 2023 due to channel destocking as distributors reduced excess inventory post-pandemic. The segment is the largest in terms of sales as it produced $2.28B in revenue for the LTM.

The company’s Herbicides segment controls weeds that hurt crop yield and harvest efficiency. It has key products such as Authority and Boral that have become vital for protecting crop yields, especially in large-scale agriculture. New formulations and tank-mix compatibility are currently driving adoption, and the sector has generated $1.26B in revenue during the LTM.

Fungicides prevent and treat fungal diseases, which are crucial in humid and wet climates. It protects yield and quality, supporting food security and farmer profitability. The segment is smaller than the two others, but it held steady during the tumultuous times of the company. Climate change could also increase fungal pressure, which would boost the need for FMC’s effective solutions. The segment generated $334.9mm in the LTM.

Plant Health is an emerging segment that includes nutritional supplements, seed treatments, and other products that align with the goal of sustainable agriculture, reducing the current reliance on synthetic chemicals. The segment generated $200 million in the LTM.

Market Dynamics

The global population growth and therefore the rising food demand boost the need for higher crop yields, which would boost the demand for FMC’s products. This would push farmers to rely on effective solutions to protect various crops such as corn, rice, and soybeans. FMC also answers the need for sustainable agriculture as it has a vast base of products that could fit the regulatory demand on that front.

Looking at regional dynamics, Latin America has faced severe destocking and drought, reducing the demand for crop protection. The weakness persisted into 2025 with Q1 sales declining. The same destocking happened in North America as lower commodity prices cut and higher interest rates curbed farmer spending.

The destocking mainly came from the fact that distributors and retailers absolutely stockpiled crop protection amid supply chain fears, and as it normalized later in the cycle, they reduced their excess inventory, therefore reducing the number of orders for FMC.

FMC also has a pipeline of new premium products, which should help FMC continue to boost its sales as older patents, such as the company’s diamide crop chemicals, roll off (diamides represented 35% of sales in 2024). Furthermore, the pipeline will tilt back to a more balanced mix as it was heavily skewed toward insecticides. Furthermore, the company has been able to extend the life of some off-patent molecules through licensing agreements such as the ones for Sulfentrazone and diamides.

Looking Ahead

For its FY 2025 financial outlook, the company is expecting around $4.15B to $4.35B in total revenue, driven by higher volume in the growth portfolio. Although this is about the same as FY 2024, customers stay cautious with their purchasing behavior. This will result in around $3.26/$3.7 EPS.

Valuation

FMC currently has a P/E GAAP of 12.47, around 44% lower than the sector median, while its EV/EBITDA is currently higher than the sector median. At 11.2, P/B is also fairly low for such a company at 1.09. All and all, valuation metrics tend to be on the lower side thanks to the company’s stock decline.

Growth has been the big question about the company, with all metrics declining over a 3-year span since the big supply push of the COVID era. Although most of FMC's peers have seen close to the same decline affecting the industry. That would be the whole case about the turnaround of the company if FMC can claw back to sustained growth despite patents rolling off with its new product pipeline and the expansion of its Plant Health portfolio. The company has the right industry tailwinds to be able to just execute well and gain back what it has lost.

Despite the reduced earnings of Q1'25, the company's margins are comparing fairly well compared to its peers, even if historically gross margins stood above 40%. The company has increased prices, but it will not be seen yet until later this year when volumes should stabilize.

Returns, however, are currently low as seen across the industry, although the company has shown strong ROEs across its lifetime. I believe again that that should turn around as the company expects peak sales for its four main new product launches to be $2B, in line to replace total diamides sales. Bans on synthetic chemicals should also boost the company's new products.

Risks

The biggest risk that could derail a turnaround investment in FMC would be the company's ability to maintain premium prices despite patents expiring. This would certainly keep revenue depressed. As mentioned, the company has a strong R&D pipeline, but there are no guarantees that it will develop into commercial successes.

Lower Prices and farmer incomes would lead to lower demand for the company's chemicals, which would again lower total sales. The industry could also be disrupted by insect-resistant genetically modified seeds in countries such as India and China. FMC is also operating in a competitive industry in which it is one of the smaller players.

Conclusion

I believe that FMC could be a good turnaround play, as I believe that the worst is behind the company. Current tailwinds such as the growing population and the need for better crop yield, plus more sustainable ways to increase outputs. I also think that the company has the pipeline to further entice sales. I would rate FMC as a buy.

data.title: Nvidia Q1: What Everyone Missed, And What They Didn't
data.description:
Introduction

The most anticipated event for the tech sector took place yesterday, and of course, I am talking about Nvidia Corporation's (NASDAQ:NVDA) Q1 2026 earnings report. Probably all of you have seen the headlines already - Nvidia posted another strong quarter, the company absorbed a $4.5 billion hit from Chinese inventories, an $8 billion headwind from export controls is expected to happen in Q2, and a margin drop that we saw this quarter.

The results were not as mind-blowing as Nvidia often presents for obvious reasons, but the stock of the company has surged for the first time after the earnings in the last year, at least. If you look back, you will see that the ridiculous beats send the stock flying down, but maybe this pattern will be broken this quarter.

In this article, I will highlight the parts of the results and the earnings call that I found the most important and interesting. I welcome any additions in the comment section from readers, as I am not focusing on every metric.

Q1 2026 Earnings Recap

Nvidia delivered $44 billion in revenue, up 69% year-over-year, with Data Center alone generating $39 billion, growing at 73% YoY. These are strong numbers, although we have seen higher growth rates from Nvidia in the past. Nvidia had been growing more than 200% in terms of revenue growth quarter over quarter, not once. Is it a problem? Yes, and no, it depends on who you ask. The growth that Nvidia has achieved is unseen, but investors are getting used to high standards very fast. So, every “old school” analyst will tell you that this growth deceleration is a natural shift that was supposed to happen a long time ago because, well, it’s impossible to grow 200% in revenue each quarter forever. But then we have a relatively small, but very loud on social media population of young analysts and investors, who were pumping the stock up, and this group will be disappointed to see something less than what they are used to.

At the same time, the company took a major hit tied to China, specifically a $4.5 billion inventory write-down related to its H20 GPUs. These chips were developed specifically for the Chinese market to comply with earlier export rules (they had some limitations, and H20 was the way to still earn some money from China), but after the U.S. tightened restrictions in April, Nvidia was suddenly banned from shipping them. The company was left sitting on billions worth of inventory and purchase obligations it could no longer fulfill. It was a huge loss for the company because it took years of development, production, and logistics. All the efforts were thrown away just because of a single regulation. This was the reason for the much lower-than-expected profitability of Nvidia, and future potential in one of the largest AI markets globally is now limited.

The company still managed to beat expectations and guided higher for Q2, projecting $45 billion in revenue, but don’t underestimate the Chinese hit. CEO Jensen Huang even said on the call that due to these regulations, the company will be losing its market share to rivals from other countries, especially from China:

China's AI moves on with or without U.S. chips. It has to compute to train and deploy advanced models. The question is not whether China will have AI, it already does. The question is whether one of the world's largest AI markets will run on American platforms. Shielding Chinese chipmakers from U.S. competition only strengthens them abroad and weakens America's position. Export restrictions have spurred China's innovation and scale. The AI race is not just about chips. It's about which stack the world runs on. As that stack grows to include 6G and quantum, U.S. global infrastructure leadership is at stake.

But let’s get back to the absorption of the regulatory hit. What made it possible was how quickly and aggressively Blackwell took over. Even though it just launched, it already makes up nearly 70% of Data Center compute revenue. They’re delivering thousands of units every week, with better yields and growing trust from all the big customers. Microsoft alone got tens of thousands of Blackwells this quarter, and they’re planning to order even more for OpenAI growth. So if you’re wondering how they still beat expectations after taking a $4.5 billion hit, this is your answer. The demand was great, and if the regulatory pressures didn’t impact the numbers, Nvidia would be in a much better place.

On average, major hyperscalers are each deploying nearly 1,000 NVL72 racks or 72,000 Blackwell GPUs per week and are on track to further ramp output this quarter. Microsoft, for example, has already deployed tens of thousands of Blackwell GPUs and is expected to ramp to hundreds of thousands of GB200s with OpenAI as one of its key customers.

But that’s not the whole story.

Some Interesting Points Worth Mentioning

Most analysts and investors were quick to notice surging AI demand, but few took the time to explain how Nvidia’s business model is changing. The company, instead of just selling chips, is now embedded in AI infrastructure as well, specifically with its NVL72 racks and Spectrum-X networking systems. These are the platforms that were built to support massive workloads. One NVL72 rack holds 72 Blackwell GPUs, and as customers generate more AI output, they need more platforms. This basically means that the more computing a customer does, the more Nvidia earns.

The rise in inference is a big part of that. We’re no longer in the era of single-prompt chatbots. AI is shifting toward what Nvidia calls agentic models (systems that analyze documents, can work with complex instructions, use external tools, and come up with sophisticated solutions). Nvidia said token usage per task has increased by as much as 1,000x.

We are witnessing a sharp jump in inference demand. OpenAI, Microsoft and Google are seeing a step function leap in token generation. Microsoft processed over 100 trillion tokens in Q1, a five-fold increase on a year-over-year basis. This exponential growth in Azure OpenAI is representative of strong demand for Azure AI Foundry as well as other AI services across Microsoft's platform.

Another important part - NVLink generated over $1 billion in revenue this quarter, and Spectrum-X is on track to bring in over $8 billion a year. Meta, Google Cloud, and other hyperscalers are already using it. These systems have become essential in how large-scale AI infrastructure is being deployed. Nvidia now controls both the compute and networking layers, and once a customer adopts that full stack, switching out becomes extremely complicated and expensive, which is great for Nvidia.

Our NVLink compute fabric in its fifth generation offers 14x the bandwidth of PCIe Gen 5. NVLink72 carries 130 terabytes per second of bandwidth in a single rack, equivalent to the entirety of the world's peak Internet traffic. NVLink is a new growth vector and is off to a great start with Q1 shipments exceeding $1 billion.

Spectrum-X posted strong sequential and year-on-year growth and is now annualizing over $8 billion in revenue. Adoption is widespread across major CSPs and consumer Internet companies, including CoreWeave, Microsoft Azure and Oracle Cloud and xAI. This quarter, we added Google Cloud and Meta to the growing list of Spectrum-X customers.

Sovereign AI is also developing fast, and I think a lot of people still don’t fully understand how important that is. Over 100 AI factories are now being built across the world, from Taiwan and Sweden to Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

The biggest news is that we are no longer talking about companies that are competing in AI. This time it’s governments, which is fascinating! This is a new, national infrastructure strategy, which, I believe, will define who moves together with time and who stays behind. If you think about this, this is very similar to how countries adopted things like electricity, telecom, or high-speed internet. Back in time, it did not look like something critical, but with time, we learned that technology can change everything. Jensen Huang described it as the next wave of infrastructure. In most of these projects, Nvidia is the platform everything else is being built around, so once again, it will be irreplaceable.

Countries are racing to build national AI platforms to elevate their digital capabilities. At COMPUTEX, we announced Taiwan's first AI factory in partnership with Foxconn and the Taiwan government. Last week, I was in Sweden to launch its first national AI infrastructure. Japan, Korea, India, Canada, France, the U.K., Germany, Italy, Spain, and more are now building national AI factories to empower startups, industries and societies. Sovereign AI is a new growth engine for Nvidia.

Financials

Let’s talk about profitability. Nvidia is still one of the strongest companies in the industry in terms of profitability. Gross margin came in at 61% on a non-GAAP basis, which looks like a disastrous drop from the 76% they reported last quarter, but there is a “but.” This quarter included $4.5 billion of unsellable inventory in China. If you exclude that, margins would have been 71.3%, which is a dip, of course, but not a substantial one. They’re guiding for 72% next quarter. So operationally, margins are holding up really well, even with everything going on geopolitically.

EBIT margin is at 62.4%, and free cash flow margin is at 33.9%. Incredibly strong numbers compared to the sector. Return on equity is at 119%, which is almost 25 times higher than the sector average. Return on total capital is 71%, which is hard to even compare to other companies. Besides, Nvidia’s profitability is improving compared to its own already high five-year average.

The Risks

The export controls are still the biggest short-term risk. The $50 billion Chinese AI market is closed to the U.S. and specifically to Nvidia right now. There’s no legal way around it and no way to replace what was lost with the H20s. That’s a huge chunk of addressable market that Nvidia won’t be able to reach anytime soon, unfortunately.

There’s also the question of whether demand can keep up. This quarter, the company was lucky to cover all of the issues by Blackwell's results, but if sovereign projects slow down or hyperscalers decide to cut spending, we could see slower growth. And then there’s U.S. politics. New regulations are being announced left and right, then being pulled back, and this doesn’t add certainty for this stock.

By the way, there was a Reuters report right before earnings suggesting Nvidia is developing a cheaper version of the H20 chip for China that could overcome U.S. export restrictions. But nothing about this was mentioned on the call, so for now, the China question is still open.

Conclusion

I would not focus too much on the revenue and the Chinese $4.5 billion hit because, in my opinion, right now, it matters less. Of course, the fact that Nvidia doesn’t have access to the Chinese market limits the company, but hopefully, this restriction will be changed, as the company will find a workaround. I think more important is that Nvidia’s entire business model is transforming, Nvidia becomes even stickier, and most people are still not talking about it.

I opened my NVDA position a while ago, and I am continuing to hold. Long term, I like where the company is headed, and I’m not planning to sell. The numbers this quarter were strong, but not blow-your-mind strong, and the fact that the stock surged is a little odd, especially since it’s usually falling after good results. I think we’ll get better entry points, especially with U.S. politics making the whole sector so volatile. If you're long, this quarter supports your hold approach. If you are looking to invest, I think it’s best to stay out for now, as in my opinion, you will have a chance to catch a better price.

data.title: AbbVie: Fairly Valued, But Lacking A Spark
data.description:
In March 2025, I wrote my last article about AbbVie Inc. (NYSE:ABBV) as management announced it will also try to enter the obesity market. Of course, I did not only focus on the opportunities the obesity market offers for any pharmaceutical business, but also rated the company as a “Hold” because the stock was fairly valued in my opinion. In the conclusion I wrote:

In my opinion, AbbVie remains a “Hold” and is more or less fairly valued at this point. The company trying to enter the obesity market does not change that assessment because when the obesity drug(s) of AbbVie will contribute to revenue, it is not the Humira sales that need to be replaced by already the next round of pharmaceuticals losing patent protection.

And since my last article was published, the stock declined about 11%. Right now, the stock is trading about 15.5% below its previous all-time high. When looking at the chart, we see several corrections around 20% for AbbVie in the last few years and in 2019, the stock declined almost 50%.

The question right now is if AbbVie is undervalued again and a solid “Buy” or if we should rather pass on the stock and maybe focus on other pharmaceutical businesses like Novo Nordisk (NVO) or Amgen (AMGN), which are also focusing on the obesity market. And as always, let’s start by looking at the last results.

Quarterly Results

On March 31, 2025, AbbVie reported first quarter results for fiscal 2025 and it beat expectations on non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.06 and also beat revenue expectations by $420 million. When looking at the results year-over-year, net revenue increased from $12,310 million in Q1/24 to $13,343 million in Q1/25 – resulting in 8.4% year-over-year growth. While the top line increased in the low-single digits, operating earnings increased 33.4% year-over-year from $2,798 million in the same quarter last year to $3,733 million in this quarter.

However, diluted earnings per share declined 6.5% year-over-year from $0.77 in Q1/24 to $0.72 in Q1/25. Instead of GAAP earnings per share, we can also look at adjusted, non-GAAP earnings per share, which increased from $2.31 in Q1/24 to $2.46 in Q1/25 – resulting in 6.5% year-over-year growth.

Not only did the company report solid results in the first quarter, it also raised its guidance for the full year of 2025. Adjusted diluted earnings per share for the full year 2025 are now expected to be in a range of $12.09 to $12.29 – 10 cents higher compared to the previous guidance. Guidance for revenue was also raised by $700 million and management is now expecting annual sales to be around $59.7 billion.

Immunology

When looking at the different products of AbbVie, Immunology is probably the most important among the major categories. In the first quarter of fiscal 2025, Immunology generated $6,264 million in revenue and is therefore responsible for 47% of total revenue. We still see the shift from Humira to Skyrizi and Rinvoq going on and Humira sales declined 50.6% year-over-year to $1,121 million in the first quarter. These are still steep declines we are seeing, but considering peak sales of $22 billion in 2021, Humira is now relatively close to zero and AbbVie managed to replace these sales quite well.

The two drivers of growth, which are offsetting the decline are Rinvoq sales, which increased 57.2% year-over-year to $1,718 million and Skyrizi sales, which increased 70.5% year-over-year to $3,425 million.

Last year, AbbVie provided a new long-term guidance for Skyrizi and Rinvoq and expected about $27 billion in combined sales. But I mentioned already in my previous article that management has update the guidance in the meantime. During the previous earnings call, CEO Rob Michael stated:

We anticipate a substantial portion of this growth will be driven by robust performance from Skyrizi and Rinvoq to assets are expected to collectively generate nearly $24 billion of revenue in 2025, reflecting growth of more than $6 billion. Based on this strong momentum, we now expect Skyrizi and Rinvoq to exceed more than $31 billion of combined sales in 2027, which is $4 billion above the guidance we provided last year.

In 2027, AbbVie is expecting Skyrizi sales to be around $20 billion and sales for Rinvoq to be around $11 billion. Analysts are expecting even slightly higher peak sales (but only in 2030) and assume Rinvoq and Skyrizi will reach $32 billion in sales.

And for 2025, management also updated the guidance, and the increase in sales will stem from Skyrizi, which is expected to generate $16.5 billion in revenue (an increase of $600 million to previous guidance) and from Rinvoq, which is expected to generate $8.2 billion in revenue (an increase of $300 million). However, Humira guidance was lowered by $500 million and full-year revenue is expected to be only $3.5 billion now.

Additionally, AbbVie also has announced its intent to acquire Nimble Therapeutics in December 2024 and closed the acquisition in January 2025. The lead asset of the company is an investigational oral peptide IL23R inhibitor. It is currently in preclinical development and will be for the treatment of psoriasis. Under the terms of the agreement “AbbVie will make a cash payment of $200 million at closing to acquire Nimble, subject to certain customary adjustments, in addition to certain interim funding payments. Nimble's shareholders remain eligible for a potential payment, subject to the achievement of a development milestone.“

Obesity

Another potential growth market in the future might be obesity. As I wrote in my last article, AbbVie only recently entered this market but management is also underlining the huge potential it sees in this market:

Obesity represents a significant global health concern with high unmet need. This market will continue to evolve with improved offerings, and we believe our program with Gubra has the potential to deliver a differentiated asset. Going forward, we plan to invest further in obesity along with other opportunities across our existing five key growth areas.

Without much doubt, the obesity market is offering huge growth potential in the coming years although Goldman Sachs recently lowered its expectations for the years to come. Nevertheless, the company is expecting global sales as high as $120 billion in ten years from now.

While Goldman Sachs is getting a little more cautious, Morgan Stanley on the other hand is getting more optimistic. Instead of a previous estimate of $105 billion for sales in 2035, the company is now expecting sales as high as $150 billion in ten years from now.

And during the last earnings call, AbbVie management also provided some updates:

In obesity, our partner, Gubra recently announced positive interim results from the first part of a multiple ascending dose study for our long acting amylin analog ABBV-295. This initial phase of the study tested one and two milligrams dosed once weekly for six weeks in healthy, lean and overweight patients. The study showed 295 performed well, demonstrating a dose dependent mean weight loss compared to placebo and a tolerability profile consistent with the results from the single ascending dose study. Mean weight loss in the two milligram cohort, which had a mean BMI of 24 was 7.8% compared to a weight gain of 2% in the placebo arm on day 43. The second phase of this study is ongoing and is evaluating higher doses in overweight and obese patients with 12 week dosing. Titration and longer dosing intervals will also be assessed. Full data from this part of the study are expected next year.

Despite management being optimistic and obesity drugs having the potential to contribute billions of dollars to AbbVie’s top line, we should not ignore that AbbVie is facing tough competition from Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk. Additionally, the drugs are in a very early stage: Not only can a lot go wrong, but it will take several years before we could see a meaningful contribution to revenue.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step we are calculating an intrinsic value once again. We can start by looking at the valuation multiples – especially the price-earnings ratio as well as the price-free-cash-flow ratio. When looking at the price-earnings ratio, AbbVie is currently trading for 78 times earnings and in the last few quarters, the valuation multiple constantly increased.

When looking for a reason for these constantly increasing valuation multiples, declining earnings per share in the last three years certainly contributed. Of course, we can also look at the adjusted earnings per share (for fiscal 2024, it was $10.12) and we get a price-earnings ratio of 18, which seems much more reasonable.

The better metric is anyway the price-free-cash-flow ratio and right now, AbbVie is trading for 21.3 times free cash flow. And now it depends somewhat on the perspective if we see AbbVie as rather expensive or not. Compared to the average P/FCF ratio of the last 10-years (13.75), AbbVie seems to be trading for high valuation multiples right now. However, for a business growing in the mid-single digits (or even higher) a valuation multiple of 20 seems acceptable.

While simple valuation metrics can give us a first hint if a stock is a bargain or not, we are calculating an intrinsic value for the stock by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate (the annual return we like to achieve) and with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (which was 1,772 million). And as basis for our calculation, we take the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $15.38 billion.

Calculating with these assumptions and a share price of $185 right now, AbbVie has to grow its free cash flow about 8% annually for the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity.

And at least for the next 8 years analysts are expecting earnings per share to increase with a CAGR of 8.52% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2032. I know, this has some tendency of cherry-picking because when looking at the years fiscal 2033 and fiscal 2034, analysts are expecting declining earnings per share again. Nevertheless, growth rates in the mid-to-high single digits seem achievable as AbbVie was able to grow revenue and operating income in the double-digits (EPS is fluctuating a little bit).

However, we can also point out that free cash flow was as high as $24.8 billion only about two years ago and right now, “only” 26.8% of revenue is ending up as free cash flow. In the past that number was as high as 44.8% and while it is not the best strategy to take the highest number ever reported, it makes sense to look at the average. But the average was also about 700 basis points higher than the current free cash flow margin. Putting the pieces together, I think we can see AbbVie as fairly valued.

Conclusion

Although AbbVie seems to be more reasonably valued right now than when my last article was published, I would still rate the stock as a “Hold” and not as a “Buy”. If someone is buying AbbVie at this point, I won’t call it a bad idea, however I think there are better investments in the market right now.

And AbbVie, as a pharmaceutical company is probably more recession-resilient than companies from most other sectors and considering the rather high probability for a recession in the United States, it makes sense to look for stable companies. Additionally, AbbVie is paying a dividend yield of 3.6% while holding the stock.

data.title: Imperial Brands: Neutral Rated As Valuation Is At Where It Should Be
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a hold rating for Imperial Brands (OTCQX:IMBBY) as the 1H25 results, while encouraging on execution, have areas that I find concerning. Yes, pricing remains strong, market share is up in key markets, and NGP losses are narrowing. But revenue momentum in NGP is mixed, market share gains are uneven, and tobacco volumes continue to decline. With the stock trading at ~8.5x forward earnings—roughly in line with its 10-year average—I don’t think there’s enough upside to get excited here.

Company Description

IMBBY is a UK-based tobacco company that is the fourth-largest cigarette manufacturer internationally. Its legacy cigarette brands include Davidoff, Gauloises, and Winston. The new strategy IMBBY deploys today is to push into the next-generation products (NGP) space, particularly with its blu e-vapor brand. IMBBY's core business is split into Tobacco and NGP, which includes manufacturing, marketing, and sales of both traditional and new products, and Distribution, which runs through Logista Integral (IMBBY owns just over half of Logista), which is publicly traded in Spain and handles both IMBBY and third-party products across Europe.

1H25 Earnings Review

IMBBY 1H25 results largely met expectations, with reported revenue coming in at GBP14.6 billion, driven by core business (tobacco and NGP) net revenue growth of 3.2% y/y in constant currency (CC) to GBP3.66 billion. Pricing was the key driver here, while volume and FX were headwinds. Within tobacco, Europe saw 1.1% net revenue growth despite a 2.2% volume decline; the Americas saw 4.9% revenue growth due to a very strong 10.1% price/mix contribution; and the AAACE region saw 2.5% growth driven mostly by price/mix. As for NGP, it grew 15.4% y/y CC to GBP160 million. Europe led the growth with 17.2% y/y, while the Americas grew 60% thanks to Zone’s (this is IMBBY’s modern oral nicotine pouch) momentum. AAACE was weaker, down 58.3% due to regulatory issues and shipment timing. As for Logista, the distribution arm, it saw 5.6% gross profit y/y growth CC to GBP764 million, but EBIT was flat y/y at GBP166 million.

At the group level, adj. EBIT grew 1.8% to GBP1.65 billion, and adj. EPS grew 6% to 123.9 pence. Overall, I thought the 1H25 results reinforce the same narrative that IMBBY continues to offset volume declines with pricing gains, gradually scaling NGP and keeping profitability intact.

Positive Operational Highlights and Strategic Execution

My positive takeaway from the 1H25 results is beyond the headline numbers noted above. The real positive takeaway, I think, is the solid progress in execution, particularly in its core tobacco business. The biggest win was market share—IMBBY grew aggregate share across its five priority markets (US, Germany, UK, Spain, and Australia) by 6 bps. In particular, Germany was a highlight with a 65 bps gain, marking this the first material share gain there in over a decade. This share gain is very encouraging because it shows that the management strategy of more targeted brand-building and reinvestment in the sales force is working. Aside from validating that the strategy is working, the most important takeaway here is this gives IMBBY more pricing power (to reiterate, tobacco price/mix grew 5.9% y/y and was the key driver behind the 2.7% revenue growth in constant currency). In my view, because of the brand-building work IMBBY has been doing over the past few years, it has helped to build up a more loyal customer base, and this is the holy grail to set up for more price increases over the long term.

NGP also showed momentum. Segment revenue grew 15.4% y/y CC and adj. EBIT losses narrowed by 14% y/y to GBP43 million, and this is despite investments ramping up. In the US, Zone now has a 2.6% national share and >5% in its own retail footprint and has >70,000 store placements secured in just a year. This is a very impressive start, which instills confidence that this growth momentum could continue to go on, as there is a strong secular tailwind in modern oral nicotine pouch demand. Using the execution success seen in heated tobacco, where Pulze 2.0 is gaining traction in Italy, reaching 3.3% share (share up 90 bps), I am inclined to think IMBBY has a product that fits the market, and given that NGP has been a key growth driver for many tobacco companies, I believe IMBBY is making the right decision here.

Challenges and Areas to Monitor

Now, while I like the execution, I have reasons to believe now is not the time to invest in the stock. IMBBY’s NGP segment still needs to prove its worth. Yes, revenue grew 15.4% y/y in CC, losses narrowed meaningfully, and the market share gains are encouraging, but revenue growth did not match up to the hype. Europe NGP sales growth slowed 900 bps from 22% in 2H24; the Americas NGP revenue growth of 60% y/y slowed dramatically from 101% in 2H24; and the AAACE region saw a sharp 58.3% y/y revenue decline to just GBP5 million (due to regulatory shifts in Poland, shipment timing issues, and an exit from the Czech Republic). The latter is worrisome because it shows that despite all the product innovation and brand building, sales can still be down sharply and there is still a lot of demand volatility. The regulatory shift is outside of IMBBY’s control and could hit every possible region, and the exit from the Czech Republic shows that even with strong secular tailwinds, there just may not be enough demand yet.

On the combustible tobacco side, I really liked that IMBBY gained market share across its five priority markets overall, but this does not take away my attention from the fact that both Spain and the UK posted share losses—down 90 bps and 70 bps, respectively. Management was upfront about this being a “deliberate pricing action,” in that they chose to prioritize value over volume. That makes sense from a brand standpoint, but it also shows how vulnerable IMBBY's market share position is. As a sin, in markets facing inflationary or regulatory pressure (forced to raise prices due to duty tariffs), IMBBY could continue to see market share loss if it wants to protect its brand’s value. As such, I am not ready to be very bullish about IMBBY's share gains in its five markets just yet until I see that it is able to sustain that share level.

Taking a step back, I am also wary and worried about the secular volume decline theme. Volumes fell 3.2% y/y in 1H25, and it's especially tough in developed markets. The US remains particularly tough due to increasing illicit vape use and weak consumer spending, and management acknowledged there haven’t been any meaningful improvements in both issues. Australia's volumes also fell sharply due to high excise taxes and illegal trade. Even the UK saw a steep volume decline, driven by duty hikes. As such, while IMBBY group revenue is still holding up well thanks to pricing, this volume drag narrative will remain as a structural issue and I think will act as a major overhang on the stock’s valuation for a very long time.

Finally, the CEO transition adds another layer of uncertainty. Stefan Bomhard will step down on October 1 after a five-year tenure, and this is surprising, especially since he recently announced the 2030 strategy. The current CFO, Lukas Paravicini, will take over as CEO, with Strategy Chief Murray McGowan moving into the CFO role. As much as I think these two personnel are solid enough to run the show, leadership transitions always carry some risk in terms of execution and culture fit.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, I don’t think it screams cheap enough to justify “ignoring” the concerns I have and giving a buy rating. It trades at 8.5x forward PE, which is the historical average for the past 10 years. This seems to be a fair multiple given the solid execution it has demonstrated, offset by the concerns I have—NGP revenue growth, market share gains sustainably, and structural headwinds in tobacco volume. Its closest peer—British American Tobacco (BATS)—is also trading at a similar range, at 10x forward PE, which I think the premium is fair since BATS is expected to grow revenue faster than IMBBY (mid-single-digit 2-year CAGR vs. IMBBY at low-single-digit).

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for IMBBY. Execution is clearly improving, and management deserves credit for scaling new growth areas. But in my view, the stock is fairly valued, trading at a 10-year average multiple, and lacks a near-term catalyst for rerating. Unless NGP growth reaccelerates or volumes start to stabilize, it’s hard to argue for anything more than a market-perform view. I’ll revisit if momentum improves, but for now, the risk/reward looks balanced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 221: $22,100 Allocated, $2,225.40 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was another rough week for the markets as the S&P 500 closed down -1.85% and the Nasdaq fell -1.51%. The weakness started with Moody's downgrading U.S. debt from Aaa to Aa1 due to large fiscal deficits, which led to a spike in treasury yields. Treasury yields spiked again after President Trump's proposed tax-cut bill gained more steam and reports surfaced that it could add $3 trillion to the deficit over the next decade. The U.S. and China reached an agreement to slash tariffs for 90 days, which boosted morale across the markets. We will see what happens when the tax-cut bill goes through the Senate and what implications this will have on businesses and the deficit. We have a FOMC meeting approaching starting on June 18th, and CME Group is projecting that there is a 97.8% chance that rates remain unchanged. I think the Fed will wait and see what the next set of CPI and PPI numbers look like, as well as any movement on trade deals, before starting to cut again. While I believe the Fed should have already started their cutting cycle due to unemployment continuing to increase, I don't think they're going to start cutting until September at this point. There is a lot of uncertainty, and the tax-bill may add too much fuel to the fire, but the driving factor that not enough people are discussing will likely be unemployment. We need unemployment to decline, and to do that, and we need borrowing costs to decline so more entrepreneurs can start businesses and existing businesses are incentivized to expand. I am going to take this opportunity to continue adding too many positions across the portfolio, as I still believe the market will do very well in the 2nd half of the year and that there are many opportunities to allocate capital.

The market retraced this week, especially after Moody's decreased the rating on the U.S. debt. After allocating $22,100 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 221 at $27,300.97. This was a weekly decline of -2.11% (-$440.62), taking the account's profitability to $5,200.97, which is a return on invested capital of 23.53%. This week, I added to my positions in the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and BP p.l.c. (BP). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $19.69 of dividend income in week 221. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $11.64 (0.53%) to $2,225.40. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,059.77 of income since inception, which is 18.37% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The monthly dividend income continues to increase as I am constantly allocating capital toward the portfolio. The dividend snowball continues to grow, and every dollar I have generated in dividend income has been reinvested back into the underlying positions. It's not even June, and I am getting close to reaching $2,250 of dividend income. I am looking forward to evaluating my initial 2025 projections, as I think that I will be exceeding $2,500 of forward income by the time 2025 ends. I am looking forward to seeing how the portfolio progresses because there are many companies that should do well when the rate-cutting cycle begins.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

This is not a portfolio focused on capital appreciation, and I am very happy with the performance. The goal has been to build an income-producing portfolio from the ground up, brick by brick. I started the portfolio with $100 and have added $100 each week while documenting every dividend generated and where every dollar has been allocated. My investment parameters have been to not exceed a 5% portfolio weighting in a single position or 20% for a sector. The portfolio goals are to generate recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation is a secondary goal that, I believe, will be achieved. When I look at the chart below, my investment goals are being met as the downside has never been exaggerated too far to the downside while the portfolio has been able to navigate every macroeconomic environment and geopolitical headwind that has been thrown at it. I believe the portfolio will continue doing well, and I am excited to continue allocating capital toward it.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $671.38 (30.17%)
  • Equities $550.48 (24.74%)
  • CEFs $391.65 (17.60%)
  • REITs $368.53 (16.56%)
  • BDCs $243.36 (10.94%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 21 weeks in 2025, $881.39 of dividend income was produced, which is 54.30% of the total income from 2024. There were 287 positions that paid a dividend, which is 40.48% of the dividends generated in 2024.

It's incredible that 21 weeks have passed, and the dividend income being generated continues to impress me. This week, there was $19.69 of dividend income generated, which took my weekly average to $41.97. This represents a 34.46% increase, as an additional $10.76 of dividend income is generated on a weekly basis compared to last year. When I compare the 2 charts below, the weekly income looks completely different, and I have already had 2 weeks exceed $100 of income generated, while I only exceeded $80 once in the first 39 weeks of 2024. I think it's entirely possible that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will exceed $2,500 in forward dividend income by the end of 2025, and $125 will exceed the new high on weekly income.

We're heading into the last week of the May, and I have already generated $120.52 of dividend income this month. I am excited to see where the monthly income finishes, as I have already exceeded what was produced in May 2024. The progression is very interesting, as I have averaged a 58.57% YoY increase in monthly dividend income across the first 4 months of 2025. I want to see how close I can get to generate $200 of monthly dividend income in May, as I feel it's going to happen sooner or later. Once June is over, I will make some new predictions, as I feel I may be able to get to $225 or $250 of monthly dividend income before the end of the year.

British American Tobacco (BTI) no longer generates 1 share from its dividends, which means there are 43 positions accomplishing this goal. The new shares that are produced from these positions are expected to generate $230.08 in additional dividend income. I am continuing to work on several positions and see a lot of value in many companies within the portfolio. I still believe that the goal of having at least 50 positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends before the end of the year is possible to reach, and I plan on focusing a portion of the weekly capital toward these positions. Over the next several years, I am hoping that the table below is lopsided and there are way more companies generating at least 1 share from the income they produce than positions that aren't.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

The gap between ETFs and REITs is expanding as REITs retreat closer to 18% of the portfolio. I am glad neither of these sectors is pushing against the 20% threshold, and while I added to JEPI this week, I am trying not to allocate capital toward ETFs as much as other sectors. Individual equities now represent 39.20% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 24.74% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.80% of the portfolio and generate 75.26% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

It's incredible that Altria Group (MO) continues to remain above 4.5% of the portfolio and increased to 4.77% of the portfolio this week. I haven't added to MO since February 2024, and its share price has exceeded so much that it's overshadowing the rest of the positions. I am trying to get the top-4 positions to represent less of the portfolio, and my goal is to have all of them representing less than 3.5% of the portfolio. I think that the top-20 positions are going to change a bit over the next several months, as I see a bunch of opportunities I am looking to allocate capital toward. The long-term goal

The top-10 positions changed this week as SL Green Realty (SLG) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) were replaced by JEPI and BTI. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,410.92, and they finished the week with a value of $8,271.11. This is an ROI of $1,860.19 (29.02%). There has been $1,063.70 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 16.59% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $619.25 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.66%. These positions represent 30.30% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.83% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 221 Additions

In week 221, I added to my positions in JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF, Blue Owl Capital Corporation, and BP p.l.c.

JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF

  • I recently wrote an article discussing my investment thesis on JEPI (can be read here)
  • I wanted to add another share of JEPI heading into NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) earnings as I feel it has a good chance to lift the market and many of the underlying holdings in JEPI higher
  • JEPI has generated 59 monthly distributions since inception and paid investors $24.14, which is 48.27% of its IPO price
  • JEPI continues to deliver reliable high single-digit income, and I plan on compounding it for years to come
  • I like JEPI's portfolio, especially since they take a conservative approach compared to other high-income funds

BP p.l.c.

  • Oil is back over $60, and BP can still do well in this environment, unlike smaller upstream producers
  • I think that BP under $30 is a great opportunity, and it's currently yielding over 6.5%
  • The world is going to need more energy due to the increasing population and the number of data centers getting spun up.
  • I think that BP is a solid long-term income play that is trading at a discount to where it will be in the future

Blue Owl Capital Corporation

  • I had some capital left over this week, so I added to OBDC
  • I think OBDC will do well in the back half of 2025 because when the Fed starts cutting rates, it should see increased originations across its loan book
  • OBDC yields over 10%, and I want to lock in those yields while I still can

Week 222 Game Plan

Next week I am planning on adding to NNN REIT (NNN), the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), and the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG).

Conclusion

So far, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has gone from allocating $100 and generating $7.44 in forward dividend income in week 1 to having allocated $22,100 and generating $2,225.40 in forward dividend income in week 221. I am generating a 10.07% yield on cost and an 8.15% portfolio yield as I am up 23.53% on my initial capital invested. My long-term strategy of building an income-producing portfolio is working, as the portfolio has mitigated downside risk well and continues to generate recurring income. I am excited to see how things progress in 2025 as the dividend snowball continues to grow each week. I am projected to generate $185.45 in monthly dividend income, and I believe that by the end of 2025, I will be exceeding $200 per week on average. I look forward to interacting with everyone in the comment section, so please leave any suggestions, questions, or feedback that you have.

data.title: FSCO: Top-Tier CEF That Delivers Capital Appreciation And Reliable Income
data.description:
Overview

Credit investments continue to grow in popularity due to their ability to serve as a hedge against the volatility of traditional equities. Rather than focusing on price growth, credit investments typically emphasize providing returns through income. However, FS Credit Opportunities (FSCO) differentiates itself with its history of providing both capital appreciation and income. I previously covered FSCO and issued a buy rating due to its strong earnings and healthy dividend coverage. Since the time of my last coverage, the macro environment has changed and the fund has released an updated report, so I wanted to revisit FSCO to see how its value proposition may have changed since then.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see how FSCO's price has increased by about 12.6%. The fund has even managed to rapidly recover from the market declined caused by President Trump's initial tariff proposals. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 26.3% over the same time frame. The main appeal of maintaining a position in FSCO is the generous starting dividend yield of about 11%. This double-digit yield in combination with the monthly payouts, makes this an enticing choice for income investors.

Despite a challenging macroeconomic environment, FSCO has continued to deliver strong results. This has caused the discount pricing to shrink over recent months. So while it may not be the best time to initiate a position from a traditional valuation sense, there are plenty of metrics that confirm FSCO can continue to provide value for shareholders. This is impressive when we consider that FSCO is still relatively new with an inception dating back to only 2022. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy and investment methodology that the fund utilizes to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most updated fund overview, FSCO has total assets under management of $2.14B that is spread across 77 different portfolio companies. The fund focuses on a diverse range of debt investments with a primary focus on the senior secured first lien structure, making up 72% of assets. This is the most ideal structure because senior secured first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This limits the overall risk of the investment for FSCO and reduces the probability that the fund loses all invested capital if a deal doesn't work out as anticipated.

The second-largest portion of FSCO's investments are structured as equity investments at 10% of assets. Equity investments sit at the bottom of the corporate capital structure and have the lowest priority for repayment, making these the riskiest form of investment. While equity investments may not produce the most consistent level of cash flows, they provide FSCO with the opportunity for capital appreciation when the valuation of the underlying borrower increases.

From a sector perspective, Consumer Services makes up the largest portion of the investments at 15%. This is followed by exposure to Healthcare Equipment and Commercial & Professional service investments, accounting for 12% and 11% of assets respectively. The combination of these sectors makes up 38% of total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on FSCO's daily price movements and total return potential.

Lastly, 69% of investments are structured on a floating rate basis. This has helped FSCO capitalize on this higher interest rate environment by essentially collecting more interest income from borrowers. The higher the interest rate, the higher the interest expense that borrowers are required to pay on the debt they hold on their balance sheet. The downside is that FSCO also utilizes leverage to help fuel portfolio growth and the debt has an effective cost of borrowing of 5.71%.

Valuation

Due to FSCO's strong performance over the last twelve months, FSCO's discount to NAV level has become considerably smaller. At the moment, FSCO now trades at a small discount to NAV of nearly 4%. For reference, FSCO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 17.2% over the last three-year period. Despite the discount levels shrinking, it may still be a solid time to initiate a long-term position. If the underlying performance, NAV growth, and earnings remain strong, it seems FSCO is quickly heading towards the premium territory within the next twelve months.

In an effort to get better clarity into the fund's performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report as of the end of 2024. Net investment income landed at $0.87 per share while net realized gains amounted to $0.07 per share. Looking at prior years, we can see how the level of net investment income has continued to rise over time, which reinforces the idea that FSCO has been able to efficiently capitalize on this higher interest rate environment and have been able to grow their base level of assets. What I really like about the fund is that net investment income has always outpaced the annual distribution paid.

This means that FSCO hasn't been reliant on net realized gains to fuel the growth of the fund. This is why FSCO is capable of preserving investor capital during market declines and has been able to keep a strong NAV consistency since its inception. Just for reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $7.30 per share
  • 2021: $7.64 per share
  • 2022: $6.33 per share
  • 2023: $6.92 per share
  • 2024: $7.15 per share

However, the latest quarterly report indicates that sales and repayments of $288M have outpaced new purchases of $163M. It seems like FSCO is remaining selective with its new investments during this period of market uncertainty linked to tariffs and interest rates. I'd prefer this selective approach because it ensures that only the highest quality borrowers become part of FSCO's portfolio.

As a result, we believe we have a solid understanding of the portfolio's first order exposure to tariffs. However, we remain cautious as second and third order effects are still uncertain and may take time to materialize depending on how the tariff situation evolves. Despite the sluggish M&A environment, we saw strong origination activity supported by an expansive sourcing network. - Nicholas Heilbut, Director of Research of FS Global Credit

Judging from the fund's consistent performance since its inception, I feel confident that FSCO will eventually trade in the premium to NAV territory. Even if the fund's net realized gains don't improve, I expect superior positive returns since the distribution is well-supported by net investment income. Looking at the performance since inception, we can see how FSCO's price has increased by more than 56.5% while the total return is more than 114%.

Risk Profile

Since a large majority of the fund operates on a floating rate basis, I believe there to be an increased risk during this period of elevated interest rates. While higher interest rates may be beneficial for net investment income levels, they can also put stress on borrowers that are underperforming. A higher cost of debt can be troublesome for borrowers that do not have consistent cash flows, which is why the higher interest rate environment can lead to an increase in defaults and non-accruals.

Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. When I previously covered FSCO, non-accruals amounted to 3% of fair portfolio value. The latest earnings call confirmed that non-accruals improved, but I struggled to find an exact figure through any of the released earnings documents. Surprisingly, non-accruals weren't talked about in detail on the latest earnings call.

We had some accretive exits during Q1. And then we also had some appreciation of certain investments, including our Lifescan Global and our Correct Care Solutions investments, two names that were previously on non-accrual, but performed very well during the quarter. - Andrew Beckman, Head of FS Global Credit

S&P Global estimates that the rate of leveraged loan defaults can rise to 1.6% in a base case scenario, assuming slower economic growth and rising prices that create headwinds for borrowers. However, S&P Global estimates that leveraged loan defaults could increase as high as 3% if the political tension from tariffs escalates. We still don't know what the ripple effects of the tariff proposals will be, so I believe that the current market conditions will lead to defaults closer to the pessimistic scenario below.

Additionally, it doesn't seem like markets expect to see any interest rate cuts soon. The Fed has decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their last meeting as they continue to observe inflationary data. I believe that the rising costs of goods will ultimately lead to higher inflation. Since inflation already sits higher than the Fed's targeted level, I don't see a scenario where any sizable interest rate cuts will happen.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0645 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.774 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, the net investment income levels have now grown to outpace this annual payout amount. Just for reference, here are the net investment income amounts since the fund's inception:

  • 2020: $0.57 per share
  • 2021: $0.56 per share
  • 2022: $0.68 per share
  • 2023: $0.77 per share
  • 2024: $0.87 per share

Looking back over the fund's history, we can see that the distributions have been gradually increased since inception. The combination of strong covered from earnings and base distribution increases, reinforces that FSCO is a great option for investors seeking a source of reliable income. I only see the dividend being threatened in a scenario where interest rates are cut in the near term or if non-accruals increased. This would likely lead to lower net investment income levels at some point. However, there's a good chance that management can offset any decreases in net investment income by allocating capital towards new portfolio companies.

In addition, it's also worth mentioning that the distributions received from FSCO are typically classified as ordinary dividend income. This is expected since the distributions are funded by net investment income. Based on the amount of capital invested, your specific tax bracket, or desired income level, a position in FSCO would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on FSCO as the fund continues to demonstrate positive NAV growth and consistent growth in earnings. The fact that the fund's distributions are consistently covered by net investment income alone, takes away from the fund's reliance on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth. The strong cash generation has led to several base distribution raises since the fund's inception, making it a great choice for income investors. I do have some concerns that the higher interest rate environment can lead to more non-accruals, but the fund's consistency instills confidence that they'll be able to navigate challenging market conditions.
